headline: Young Volunteers Become Bangladesh's Secret Weapon Against Wildlife Crime
meta_description: Animal-loving volunteers across Bangladesh risk their lives to help authorities combat poaching and smuggling, leading to thousands of successful raids and ani…
summary_short: Youth volunteers in Bangladesh are playing a vital role in fighting wildlife crime, working undercover with the Forest Department to rescue thousands of animals from poa…
body:
Across Bangladesh, a network of passionate animal lovers—most of them young—has become indispensable to the government's fight against wildlife trafficking. Working without official assignment yet risking their own safety, these volunteers gather intelligence on poachers and smugglers, go undercover as buyers, and help arrest criminals. Their contributions have been so significant that in July 2026, wildlife photographer Faisal Bin Azad, one of the pioneers of this volunteer movement, was honored with the National Wildlife Conservation Award. Between July 2012 and March 2026, the Forest Department's Wildlife Crime Control Unit (WCCU) conducted nearly 3,200 raids—almost all of them planned using information provided by volunteers—rescuing over 41,000 birds, 18,000 reptiles, and more than 1,500 mammals.

Azad, who founded the conservation organization Save Wildlife and Nature in 2014, exemplifies the practical dedication these volunteers bring. In one July raid on a warehouse outside Dhaka, he and fellow volunteers spotted a Facebook advertisement for tortoises, contacted the sellers posing as customers, and then guided WCCU officials to the location, where officers arrested a smuggler and recovered 1,104 animals. "Volunteers have greater love, affection and compassion for animals than zoology students," Azad explained, noting that no special academic background is required—only genuine care and practical knowledge.

Recognizing this crucial partnership, Bangladesh's Forest Department launched a volunteer registration process in November 2025, which attracted over 1,000 interested participants within eight months. Yet some established conservation volunteers have raised concerns about whether the registration process adequately filters out potential poachers masquerading as helpers. Still, the impact is undeniable: the WCCU has arrested 222 wildlife criminals and recovered 1,406 wild trophies, achievements that would not have been possible without the volunteers working in the shadows.
quick_facts: Nearly 3,200 raids conducted since 2012, almost all planned with volunteer tips
Over 41,000 birds rescued; 18,000+ reptiles; 1,500+ mammals saved from poachers
Wildlife photographer Azad honored with National Conservation Award in July 2026
1,000+ volunteers registered in first eight months after November 2025 public notice
Volunteers work undercover and risk their lives without official government assignment
